main_summary.summary: William Lucy, longtime Secretary-Treasurer of the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME), gives a June 25, 2013 oral history to Emilye Crosby in Washington, DC about his path from Contra Costa County, California civil-service engineering aid to a central staff role in the 1968 Memphis Sanitation Strike and subsequent labor and anti-apartheid organizing. Born in Memphis under the political machine of Edward Hull "E.H." Crump, Lucy moved to Richmond, California in March 1942 when his father was recruited to Kaiser Shipyards during the World War II mobilization. After Mare Island Naval Shipyard and thirteen years at Contra Costa County (rising to system materials and research engineer), he was recruited in 1966 by AFSCME's new pro-union president Jerry Wurf to direct Legislation and Community Affairs. Sent to Memphis in February 1968 after two sanitation workers were crushed in a packer-truck mechanism, Lucy describes the 67-day strike, the "I Am a Man" slogan he helped formulate at the Peabody Hotel with a local minister, Rev. James M. Lawson Jr's role, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr's two visits and assassination on April 4, the failed earlier settlement that collapsed under the "Mayor Capitulates" headline, and Lewis Donelson's one-vote-short city council effort. He covers the founding of the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists (1972), the Free South Africa Movement (1984), and bringing Nelson Mandela on his 1990 U.S. tour.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Lucy is a primary-source narrator on the operational interior of the 1968 Memphis Sanitation Strike: who was in the room with Mayor Henry Loeb III, how the "I Am a Man" sign was drafted in the Peabody Hotel from Rev. James M. Lawson Jr's earlier press-conference framing, why the FBI under J. Edgar Hoover and provocateurs at the disrupted March 28 march figured into King's decision-making, and the mechanics of the federal mediation by Under Secretary of Labor James J. Reynolds and Frank Miles that finally produced settlement money from industrialist Abe Plough rather than the city budget.

The interview also documents the institutional history of Black labor organizing post-strike: the 1972 founding of the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists as a response to the AFL-CIO's neutrality between McGovern and Nixon, the 1984 launch of the Free South Africa Movement under Randall Robinson and TransAfrica, and the 1990 Mandela U.S. tour. As one of the few surviving senior strategists from both the Memphis strike staff and the post-Memphis Black-labor institutional build-out, Lucy's testimony connects the Civil Rights Movement's economic-justice arc to subsequent decades of trade-union and anti-apartheid work.

chapters.title: Memphis Origins and Wartime Migration
chapters.summary: Lucy describes being born in Memphis under the political machine of Edward Hull "E.H." Crump, with parents from Catherine, Alabama and Uniontown, Alabama. His father worked for Memphis Light, Gas and Water until December 1941, when wartime recruitment moved the family to Richmond, California in March 1942 to work in the Kaiser Shipyards.
related_events: Memphis Light, Gas and Water employment under Crump machine

chapters.title: Richmond, California Childhood
chapters.summary: Lucy recounts growing up in Richmond, California's wartime defense housing, attending integrated schools alongside Mexican American and African American families, and the contrast with segregated Memphis. He describes his father's mechanic business after the war, his mother's seamstress and restaurant work, and his older brother Joe Lucy Jr.

chapters.title: Contra Costa Engineering Career
chapters.summary: Lucy describes working two years at Mare Island Naval Shipyard, then thirteen years at Contra Costa County, California in the materials and research laboratory, rising from engineering aid to system materials and research engineer. He studied at UC Berkeley while working and oversaw quality control for highway construction under the Federal-Aid Highway Act of 1956.

chapters.title: Becoming a Public-Sector Trade Unionist
chapters.summary: Lucy describes joining the county employee association, the year-long debate over whether to become a true union, and the influence of older trade unionists Ben Russell and Red Aiello. The pro-union faction won the election and the group affiliated with AFSCME, which was at that time led by Arnold S. Zander but being challenged by Jerry Wurf.

chapters.title: Joining AFSCME National in Washington
chapters.summary: After Jerry Wurf's 1964 victory, Lucy was recruited to AFSCME National headquarters in Washington, DC as Associate Director of the Department of Legislation and Community Affairs in June 1966. He explains AFSCME's argument that public employees deserved the bargaining rights that the National Labor Relations Act under Franklin D. Roosevelt had specifically excluded them from.
keywords: Department of Legislation and Community Affairs
related_events: public-sector bargaining-rights exclusion under Wagner Act

chapters.title: Civil Rights Context and Minimum Wage Campaigns
chapters.summary: Lucy reflects on his awareness of the Southern Civil Rights Movement and AFSCME's work with Cesar Chavez, Larry Itliong, and the United Farm Workers in their struggle with Gallo around the grape industry. He describes the union's campaigns for survivor benefits for descendants of Panama Canal construction workers and extending minimum-wage coverage to private hospital and nursing-home workers under the interstate commerce clause.
related_events: Panama Canal construction-worker descendants benefits campaign
Marian Wright Edelman / King poor people's campaign

chapters.title: Arriving in the Memphis Sanitation Strike
chapters.summary: Lucy describes being dispatched from Detroit to Memphis in February 1968 after two sanitation workers were crushed in the packer mechanism of a garbage truck. He explains the political squeeze of a new mayor in a brand-new commission-to-mayor governance system who had won on racial polarization, the failed earlier injunction against the workers, and the Washington Post reporter coming from Marks, Mississippi (King's poor people's-campaign organizing site) who alerted AFSCME that the strike was starting.
related_events: February 1, 1968 Echol Cole and Robert Walker deaths

chapters.title: King's Involvement and the Strike Strategy
chapters.summary: Lucy describes how Dr. Martin Luther King Jr saw the strike as the contradiction of working-poor wages in a rich country and aligned it with the poor people's campaign. He explains the strategy of asking workers to talk to their wives and distribute leaflets door-to-door, the slow building of Black community support, the lukewarm white response, and the FBI under J. Edgar Hoover fostering the narrative that AFSCME had come to exploit Memphis.

chapters.title: I Am a Man and Women's Role
chapters.summary: Lucy recounts drafting the "I Am a Man" slogan with a local minister at the Peabody Hotel, building on Rev. James M. Lawson Jr's earlier framing of racism as treating a man like he is not a man. He describes the slogan's resonance with middle-aged and older workers who had gone "from boy to uncle to grandpa without ever stopping at men," and the substantial role of women in the strike's cooking, office work, and picket lines.
related_events: I Am a Man slogan creation

chapters.title: Lawson, The Invaders, and Provocateurs
chapters.summary: Lucy describes Rev. James M. Lawson Jr as a Mohandas K. Gandhi student and longtime King ally whose involvement made him the most hated African American in Memphis. He counters the narrative that the local Black youth group The Invaders fomented violence at the March 28, 1968 march, citing the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence Activities (Church Committee) findings that FBI provocateurs and outside agitators were responsible for the window-breaking.
related_events: Senate Select Committee on Intelligence Activities (Church Committee)

chapters.title: King's Assassination and Strike Resolution
chapters.summary: Lucy recounts his only direct meeting with King at the Rivermont Hotel after the disrupted March 28 march, the April 4 assassination, and the doubt about James Earl Ray acting alone. He describes the federal mediation by President Lyndon B. Johnson's appointee James J. Reynolds and Frank Miles, two earlier failed settlements (including one collapsed by a leaked "Mayor Capitulates" headline), Lewis Donelson's one-vote-short city council effort, and industrialist Abe Plough providing the settlement money that Mayor Henry Loeb III refused to put into the city budget.
related_events: I've Been to the Mountaintop speech

chapters.title: Coalition of Black Trade Unionists
chapters.summary: Lucy describes founding the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists in 1972 with Nelson Jack Edwards, Charles A. Hayes, Cleveland Robinson, and William H. "Bill" Simons as a response to the AFL-CIO's neutrality between George McGovern and Richard Nixon. He references A. Philip Randolph's earlier Negro American Labor Council, the 1,300-person founding Chicago convention, and the eventual growth to 55 chapters and a more diverse AFL-CIO executive council.

chapters.title: Free South Africa Movement and Mandela
chapters.summary: Lucy describes the 1984 founding of the Free South Africa Movement under Randall Robinson and TransAfrica during the Reagan administration to disengage U.S. policy from South African apartheid. He recounts bringing Nelson Mandela on his 1990 U.S. tour after his release from 27 years of imprisonment, and reflects on whether the United States needs a truth-and-reconciliation process comparable to South Africa's.
related_events: South Africa Truth and Reconciliation Commission
